one_line_takeaway: By reweighting DPO's preferred/dispreferred loss terms according to a mutual-information-based measure of how well the model already understands each response (with a scaling factor to preserve overall gradient magnitude), B-DPO corrects an observed 'Imbalanced Preference Comprehension' overfitting effect and raises safety scores (e.g., StrongReject 92.01% vs SafeDPO's 86.50% on Qwen2-7B-Instruct) without a meaningful drop in general-capability benchmarks.
key_finding: On the paper's own Table 1, B-DPO gives the largest reported safety gain: on Qwen2-7B-Instruct it reaches 92.01% on StrongReject versus the best baseline SafeDPO's 86.50% (a gap of 5.51 points, matching the abstract's claimed 8.01% improvement when measured relative to the plain-DPO baseline's 84.03%), plus best results on XsTest (84.50%), GCG (98.59%) and PAIR (80.00%) for that model, while GSM8K stays at 72.71% versus the base model's 71.19% (negligible alignment tax). On Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, B-DPO leads on StrongReject (73.48%), XsTest (82.50%) and PAIR (80.00%), and notably stays robust on GCG (81.82%) where SafeDPO collapses to 46.67% -- i.e., a safety method that improves average safety scores can still be individually brittle to a specific attack, and B-DPO's authors highlight this gap as evidence of more consistent robustness. The ablation (Table 2) shows the balanced weights alone raise StrongReject from DPO's 84.03% to 85.30%, the scaling factor alone to 86.58%, and both together (full B-DPO) to 92.01% -- i.e., the components are more than additive in the reported numbers, and neither component alone reaches the full gain. The diagnostic result underpinning the method (Figure 3 vs Figure 4) is that Dimbalanced achieves a *higher* reward margin during DPO training than Dbalanced, yet Dbalanced yields *better* downstream safety (+1.28% StrongReject, +4% XsTest for Mistral) and general performance (+1.35% AdvGLUE, +0.45% HHH) -- reward-margin improvement and generalization diverge, which the authors interpret as overfitting driven by comprehension imbalance rather than genuine preference-direction learning.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold. First, an empirical diagnostic finding: using mutual information I(x,y)=H(y)-H(y|x) between query x and response y as a proxy for a model's 'comprehension' of a response, the authors show that on safety preference pairs (from PKU-SafeRLHF) LLMs (Qwen-2-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) exhibit an 'Imbalanced Preference Comprehension' phenomenon: the mutual information gap between preferred (safe) and dispreferred (unsafe) responses is often large and asymmetric, and models trained on a lower-mutual-information-gap ('balanced') subset of the data generalize better on safety and general benchmarks than models trained on a higher-gap ('imbalanced') subset, even though the imbalanced subset produces a larger reward margin during DPO training (i.e., higher reward margin does not track better generalization -- a sign of overfitting to comprehension asymmetry rather than to the preference direction). Second, a method, Balanced Direct Preference Optimization (B-DPO): the standard DPO loss is reweighted per-example with weights lambda_w = 2*I(x,y_l)^alpha / (I(x,y_w)^alpha + I(x,y_l)^alpha) and lambda_l = 2*I(x,y_w)^alpha / (I(x,y_w)^alpha + I(x,y_l)^alpha) applied to the preferred/dispreferred log-ratio terms respectively (Eq. 5-6), so that the response the model already comprehends well (higher I) has its optimization pressure reduced and the less-comprehended response is compensated. Because this reweighting perturbs the DPO gradient's overall scale, the authors add a scaling factor (Eq. 8, ratio of sigma(r_l - r_w) to sigma(lambda_l r_l - lambda_w r_w), computed with a stop-gradient copy of the current parameters) to keep the total optimization pressure matched to vanilla DPO and avoid gradient oscillation.
threat_model: The paper targets safety-alignment robustness of open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs (Qwen-2-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Vicuna-7B-v1.5) against (a) direct harmful instructions (StrongReject, XsTest unsafe split) and (b) jailbreak attacks specifically GCG (transfer/optimization-based adversarial suffixes, 50 iterations over 25 AdvBench samples) and PAIR (LLM-driven iterative jailbreak refinement, 50 AdvBench samples, up to 20 iterations, Vicuna-13B-v1.5 as attacker, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct as judge for prompt construction and Qwen3-14B-Instruct as the safety judge for scoring). It is a training-time defense (safety alignment via preference optimization), not an attack paper -- GCG and PAIR are used purely as external evaluation stressors, not designed or modified by the authors.
limitations: Limitations stated by the authors themselves (Section 6): the method is developed and tested only within the DPO framework, not extended to other RLHF approaches such as PPO; and the study is confined to the textual modality, with multimodal scenarios left for future work. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own reporting (not stated by authors but visible in their data): all main experiments use a single training dataset (PKU-SafeRLHF, one epoch) and three 7B-scale open models, so scaling behavior to larger models or other alignment datasets is untested; mutual information is estimated via autoregressive entropy approximations of H(y) and H(y|x) rather than measured exactly, and no calibration/robustness analysis of this MI estimator itself is presented; the added overhead is real though modest (B-DPO: 1.9 hours vs DPO's 1.5 hours on Qwen2-7B-Instruct, with 0.4 hours attributable to the extra MI pre-computation inference pass); and safety judging for GCG/PAIR relies on an LLM-as-judge (Qwen3-14B-Instruct), inheriting whatever biases that judge has, which the paper does not separately validate.

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF (20,000-pair subset: 10,000 safe-query pairs, 10,000 unsafe-query pairs) for training and the imbalance diagnostic
AdvBench (source of 25 samples for GCG and 50 samples for PAIR jailbreak evaluation)
quantitative_results: Qwen2-7B-Instruct StrongReject: Base 80.51%, DPO 84.03%, CPO 83.71%, SimPO 78.85%, SafeDPO 86.50%, B-DPO 92.01%
Qwen2-7B-Instruct XsTest: Base 82.50%, DPO 81.50%, SafeDPO 80.50%, B-DPO 84.50%
Qwen2-7B-Instruct GCG: Base 97.37%, DPO 93.54%, SafeDPO 90.91%, B-DPO 98.59%
Qwen2-7B-Instruct PAIR: Base 58.00%, DPO 76.00%, SafeDPO 68.00%, B-DPO 80.00%
Qwen2-7B-Instruct Overall Avg (8 benchmarks): Base 68.56%, DPO 70.63%, SafeDPO 69.45%, B-DPO 73.29%
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 StrongReject: Base 47.12%, DPO 70.29%, SafeDPO 72.20%, B-DPO 73.48%
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 GCG: SafeDPO 46.67% vs B-DPO 81.82% (SafeDPO shows a large drop here despite decent average safety)
Vicuna-7B-v1.5 StrongReject: Base 67.52%, DPO 78.85%, SafeDPO 80.51%, B-DPO 85.62%
Ablation (Qwen2-7B-Instruct, StrongReject): Base 80.51%, DPO 84.03%, DPO+balanced-weights-only 85.30%, DPO+scaling-factor-only 86.58%, full B-DPO 92.01%

Balanced vs imbalanced training-data split (Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3): Dbalanced improves +1.28% on StrongReject and +4% on XsTest, and +1.35% on AdvGLUE / +0.45% on HHH, relative to Dimbalanced, despite Dimbalanced achieving a higher reward margin during training
Computational overhead (Qwen2-7B-Instruct, single RTX A800): DPO ~1.5 hours total; B-DPO ~1.9 hours total (0.4 hours for MI pre-computation inference + 1.5 hours training)
Hyperparameter alpha sweep {0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5}: alpha=1.5 selected as optimal for StrongReject safety performance (ablation, Figure 5)
baselines_compared: DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) - vanilla direct preference optimization
CPO (Xu et al., 2024a) - reference-free contrastive preference optimization combined with NLL objective
SimPO (Meng et al., 2024) - reference-free, sequence-length-normalized preference optimization
SafeDPO (Kim et al., 2025) - DPO with a safety-offset hyperparameter for unsafe queries
